g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about Three-Schema Architecture and Data Independence, a fundamental DBMS concept proposed by ANSI/SPARC in 1975.
The three-schema architecture separates user views from physical storage through three distinct levels of abstraction.
g.text: A framework that separates user views from physical storage using three abstraction levels.
Proposed by ANSI/SPARC in 1975, it achieves data independence in database systems.

g.data-narration: Data independence allows changing schema at one level without affecting higher levels, providing flexibility and stability.
g.text: Ability to modify schema at one level without impacting application programs.
Without independence, any storage change requires rewriting all applications.

g.data-narration: The three-schema architecture shows External, Conceptual, and Internal levels stacked with mappings connecting each level.
The External Level represents individual user views, showing only relevant data with multiple external schemas possible.
g.text: Each user has personalized view of data relevant to their needs.
Multiple external schemas can exist simultaneously for different user groups.
Highest abstraction level - shows only what users need to see.

g.data-narration: The Conceptual Level represents the community view of the entire database with one schema defining all entities and relationships.
g.text: Describes entire database structure: entities, attributes, relationships, constraints.
Independent of any physical storage implementation.
Only ONE conceptual schema per database - serves as bridge between levels.

g.data-narration: The Internal Level describes physical storage details including file structures, indexing methods, and access paths.
g.text: Describes how data is physically stored and accessed on secondary storage.
Includes file structures, indexing methods, data compression, access paths.
Lowest level of abstraction - most detailed view of storage.

g.data-narration: Mappings transform requests and data between schema levels: External-Conceptual and Conceptual-Internal mappings.
g.text: Transforms user requests to database operations
Translates logical to physical storage operations

g.data-narration: Data independence is the ability to change schema at one level without affecting the schema at the next higher level.
g.text: The ability to change schema definition at one level without affecting the schema
at the next higher level. Provides application stability and flexibility.
Higher separation between levels = Greater data independence

g.data-narration: Physical data independence allows changing internal schema without affecting conceptual schema - easier to achieve.
g.text: Ability to change internal schema without affecting conceptual schema.
Modify storage structures (file organizations, indexes, storage devices) without
requiring changes to logical schema or application programs.

g.data-narration: Logical data independence allows changing conceptual schema without affecting external schemas - harder to achieve.
g.text: Ability to change conceptual schema without affecting external schemas.
Add/modify entities, attributes, or relationships without impacting user views

g.data-narration: Consider a University Database: Internal schema stores data using B+ tree indexed files with sequential organization.
g.text: Internal: Data stored in B+ tree indexed files on disk.
Student table: sequential file with clustered index on Student_ID

g.data-narration: The conceptual schema defines entities: Student with ID, Name, DOB, Department, CGPA and Course with code, name, credits.
g.text: ENTITY Student { Student_ID: INTEGER (PK), Name: VARCHAR(50),
Date_of_Birth: DATE, Department: VARCHAR(30), CGPA: DECIMAL(3,2) }
ENTITY Course { Course_Code: VARCHAR(10) (PK), Course_Name: VARCHAR(50),

g.data-narration: External schemas show different views: Student view sees name, course, grade while Admin sees all details.
g.text: All student details, course details, enrollment stats

g.data-narration: Physical independence: changing storage from sequential to RAID needs no schema change. Logical: adding Department entity keeps existing views working.
Key points: Three levels - ECI order, one conceptual schema but multiple external, mappings enable independence.
g.text: • Three levels in order: External → Conceptual → Internal (ECI acronym)
• Only ONE conceptual schema per database, but MULTIPLE external schemas

g.data-narration: Avoid confusion: External is abstract user view, Internal is detailed physical storage. Get data independence direction correct.
g.text: 1. Confusing schema levels - External = user view, Internal = physical storage
2. Wrong number: ONE conceptual, MULTIPLE external schemas
3. Data independence direction: Physical protects Conceptual, Logical protects External

g.data-narration: Remember: ANSI/SPARC 1975 proposed this architecture. Physical independence is easier to achieve than logical independence.
g.text: • Physical data independence is easier to achieve than logical data independence
• Conceptual schema serves as bridge between external and internal levels
